revision.text:
{{Boxboxtop|Tada Jinja}} [[image:Tada Jinja.jpg|thumb|250px]] {{Boxboxbottom}}
'''Tada Jinja''' (多田神社 ''tada-jinja'') is a shinto shrine in [[Kawanishi, Hyogo|Kawanishi]], [[Hyogo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is famous
==History== This shrine was originally established in 970 and dedicated to Minamonto Mitsunaka, a Lord of Settsu Province as a Buddhism temple called ‘Tadain’.
==Objects for Worship== The principal objects of worship at this shrine are five people of [[Genji]] clan. [[Minamomto Mitsunaka]], [[Minamoto Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto Yorimitsu]], [[Minamoto Yoriyoshi]], and [[Minamoto Yoriie]]. Mitsunaka, Yorinobu, Yoriyoshi and Yoriie are direct ancestors of [[Minamoto Yoritomo]], the first [[shogun]] of [[Kamakura Bakufu]].
==Cultural Properties== This shrine has three important cultural properties selected by Japanese government. *The inner shrine *The outer shrine *The Zuishinmon Gate
==Access== *[[Tada Station]] of [[Nose Railway]]
[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]]
[[ja:多田神社]]

{{Boxboxtop|Tada Jinja}} [[image:Tada Jinja.jpg|thumb|250px]] {{Boxboxbottom}}
'''Tada Jinja''' (多田神社 ''tada-jinja'') is a shinto shrine in [[Kawanishi, Hyogo|Kawanishi]], [[Hyogo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is known as the ‘clan’s shrine’ of the [[Seiwa Genji|Genji]] clan which has produced many [[shogun]]s in centuries.
==History== This shrine was originally established in 970 and dedicated to Minamonto Mitsunaka, a Lord of Settsu Province as a Buddhism temple called ‘Tadain’.
==Objects for Worship== The principal objects of worship at this shrine are five people of [[Seiwa Genji]] clan. [[Minamomto Mitsunaka]], [[Minamoto Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto Yorimitsu]], [[Minamoto Yoriyoshi]], and [[Minamoto Yoriie]]. Mitsunaka, Yorinobu, Yoriyoshi and Yoriie are direct ancestors of [[Minamoto Yoritomo]], the first shogun of [[Kamakura Bakufu]].
==Cultural Properties== This shrine has three important cultural properties selected by Japanese government. *The inner shrine *The outer shrine *The Zuishinmon Gate
==Access== *[[Tada Station]] of [[Nose Railway]]
==References== *[http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp/yuisyo-e.html] Official Homepage of Tada Jinja
[[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]]

{{Boxboxtop|Tada Jinja}} [[image:Tada Jinja.jpg|thumb|250px]] {{Boxboxbottom}}
'''Tada Jinja''' (多田神社 ''tada-jinja'') is a shinto shrine in [[Kawanishi, Hyogo|Kawanishi]], [[Hyogo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is known as the ‘clan’s shrine’ of the [[Seiwa Genji|Genji]] clan which has produced many [[shogun]]s in centuries. This shrine is one of [[Three Genji Shrines]], with [[Rokuron'no Jinja]] in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]] and [[Tsuboi Hachimangu]] in [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]].
==History== This shrine was originally established in 970 and dedicated to [[Minamonto no Mitsunaka]], a Lord of [[Settsu Province]] as a Buddhism temple called ‘Tadain’.
==Objects for Worship== The principal objects of worship at this shrine are five people of [[Seiwa Genji]] clan. Minamomto no Mitsunaka, [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]]. Mitsunaka, Yorinobu, Yoriyoshi and Yoriie are direct ancestors of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], the first shogun of [[Kamakura Bakufu]].
==Cultural Properties== This shrine has three important cultural properties selected by Japanese government. *The inner shrine *The outer shrine *The Zuishinmon Gate
==Access== *[[Tada Station]] of [[Nose Railway]]
==References== *[http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp/yuisyo-e.html] Official Homepage of Tada Jinja

This shrine is one of [[Three Genji Shrines]], with [[Rokuronno Shrine]] in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]] and [[Tsuboi Hachimangu]] in [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]].
==History== This shrine was originally established in 970 and dedicated to [[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], a Lord of [[Settsu Province]] as a Buddhism temple called ‘Tadain’.
==Objects for Worship== The principal objects of worship at this shrine are five people of [[Seiwa Genji]] clan. Minamomto no Mitsunaka, [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]]. Mitsunaka, Yorinobu, Yoriyoshi and Yoshiie are direct ancestors of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], the first shogun of [[Kamakura Bakufu]].
==Cultural Properties== This shrine has three [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] selected by Japanese government. *The inner shrine *The outer shrine *The Zuishinmon Gate
==Access== *[[Tada Station]] of [[Nose Railway]]
==References== *[http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp/yuisyo-e.html] Official Homepage of Tada Jinja

revision.comment: added detail and citation
revision.text:
[[Image:Tada Jinja.jpg|thumb|250px|Tada Jinja]]
{{Nihongo|'''Tada Shrine'''|多田神社|Tada Jinja}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Kawanishi, Hyogo|Kawanishi]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is the shrine of the [[Seiwa Genji|Genji]] clan which has produced many [[shogun]]s in centuries. This shrine is one of [[Three Genji Shrines]], with [[Rokuronno Shrine]] in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]] and [[Tsuboi Hachimangu]] in [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]].
==History== This shrine was originally established in 970 and dedicated to [[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], a Lord of [[Settsu Province]] as a Buddhist temple called ‘Tadain’.
Minamoto no Mitsunaka, converted to buddhism following the visit of Ingen (954-1028), [[Genshin]] (942-1017), and Kaku'ub, of the [[Tendai]] Sect. The new convert declared, "You have shown me such extraordinary virtuous things. I'm someone who has killed an immeasurable number of living things. I'd like to atone for these sins. By building a Buddhist hall I will stop committing any more crimes and save the lives of living things." "What is known as Tada Temple is a cluster of halls that began to be built with this one."<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |page=22-29}}</ref>
==Objects for Worship== The principal objects of worship at this shrine are five people of [[Seiwa Genji]] clan. Minamoto no Mitsunaka, [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]]. Mitsunaka, Yorinobu, Yoriyoshi and Yoshiie are direct ancestors of [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], the first shogun of [[Kamakura Bakufu]].
==Cultural Properties== This shrine has three [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]] selected by Japanese government. *The inner shrine *The outer shrine *The Zuishinmon Gate
==Access== *[[Tada Station (Hyōgo)|Tada Station]] of [[Nose Railway]]
==References== *[http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp/yuisyo-e.html] Official site of Tada Jinja
{{coord|34|51|40.6|N|135|24|9.45|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kawanishi, Hyōgo}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{Nihongo|'''Tada Shrine'''|多田神社|Tada Jinja}} | image = Tada Jinja.jpg | caption = Tada Jinja | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|51|40.6|N|135|24|9.45|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Tada Shrine'''|多田神社|Tada Jinja}} is a Shinto shrine in [[Kawanishi, Hyogo|Kawanishi]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is the shrine of the [[Seiwa Genji|Genji]] clan which has produced many ''[[shōgun]]s'' in centuries. This shrine is one of [[Three Genji Shrines]], with [[Rokusonnō Shrine]] in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]] and [[Tsuboi Hachimangu]] in [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]].
==History== This shrine was originally established in 970 and dedicated to [[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], a Lord of [[Settsu Province]] as a Buddhist temple called Tadain.

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Tada Shrine
多田神社 | image = Tada-jinja honden.jpg | caption = ''Honden'' of Tada Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hyogo Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|51|40.6|N|135|24|9.4|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Tada Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 978 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Tadain Tadadokorocho, Kawanishi-shi, Hyōgo-ken 666-0127 | website = {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 10 and October 27 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tada Shrine'''|多田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Tadadokoro neighborhood of the city of [[Kawanishi, Hyōgo|Kawanishi]] in [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is the shrine of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan from which [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and many subsequent [[Shogun]] and ''[[daimyō]]'' clans claimed descent. This shrine is one of [[Three Genji Shrines]], with [[Rokusonnō Shrine]] in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]] and [[Tsuboi Hachimangū]] in [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]]. The shrine was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1951.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1877|title=多田神社|trans-title= Tada Jinja|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tada Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]]'''| 源満仲|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Tada Daigongen'''| 多田大権現|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]]'''| 源頼光 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]'''| 源頼信 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]]'''| 源頼義 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriie]]'''| 源義家 |}} ==History== In 970, [[Settsu Province|Settsu-no-kami]] [[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], the founder of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan, received an oracle from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha|Sumiyoshi Ōkami]] to established his residence in what is now Kawanishi. A temple (commonly known as Tada-in) was built, as Mitsunaka, converted to Buddhism following the visit of Ingen (954–1028), [[Genshin]] (942–1017), and Kaku'ub, of the [[Tendai]] sect. The new convert declared, "You have shown me such extraordinary virtuous things. I'm someone who has killed an immeasurable number of living things. I'd like to atone for these sins. By building a Buddhist Hall I will stop committing any more crimes and save the lives of living things." "What is known as Tada-in is a cluster of halls that began to be built with this one."{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |page=22-29}} When Mitsunaka died in 997 he was buried in Tada-in. In the [[Kamakura period]], the temple was regarded as the ancestral mausoleum of Seiwa-Genji, but it gradually declined. In the middle of the Kamakura period, [[Hojo Yasutoki]], the ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura Shogunate]], became the land steward of Tada-sho ''[[shōen]]'', he decided to carry out a large-scale reconstruction of the Tada-in. In 1273, [[Ninsho]] of [[Saidai-ji]] was appointed to undertake the restoration, and as a result, the temple changed from the Tendai sect to the [[Shingon Risshu]] sect. In the [[Muromachi period]], [[Ashikaga Takauji]], who was a descendent of the Seiwa Genji, revered the Tada-in, and temple became a mortuary temple for the [[Ashikaga shogunate]], with the ashes of successive generations of [[shogun]] buried at the temple. However, the temple was destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]] in 1577. It remained in ruins until restored in 1665 by [[Sakakibara Tadatsugu]] of [[Himeji Domain]], [[Inaba Masanori]] of [[Odawara Domain]] and the descendants of the Tada-in retainers. Shogun [[Tokugawa Ietsuna]] granted an estate with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep, and most of its structures were rebuilt by 1667. As with the Ashikaga, successive generation of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa shogun]]s ashes and ''[[ihai]]'' mortuary tablets with enshrined at the temple. Shogun [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] restored the main shrine building in 1695. In the [[Meiji period]], due to the [[shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shintoism and Buddhism]] by the government, Tada-in was transformed into a [[Shinto shrine]] enshrining Minamoto no Mitsunaka, [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]], [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] as ''[[kami]]''. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Gallery== File:Tada-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|Haiden（ICP） File:Tada-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon（ICP） File:Tada-jinja, nandaimon.jpg|MInami Daimon（兵庫県指定文化財） File:Tada-jinja, higashimon.jpg|Higashi-mon（兵庫県指定文化財） File:Tada-jinja, nishimon.jpg|Nishi-mon（兵庫県指定文化財） File:Tada-jinja, otorii.jpg|Ōtorii File:Tada-jinja byo.jpg|thumb|Grave of Minamoto no Yorimitsu ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Honden|多田神社 本殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156915|title=多田神社 本殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Haiden|多田神社 拝殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/186526|title=多田神社 拝殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Zuishimon|多田神社 随神門||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/123387|title=多田神社 随神門|trans-title=Tada Jinja Zuishinmonlanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Bunsho|多田神社文書||}}, 43 scrolls with 492 document{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/206080|title=多田神社文書|trans-title=Tada Jinja Bunsholanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ===National Tangible Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Treasury|多田神社宝物殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/442906|title=多田神社宝物殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja homotsuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Tada Shrine}} * {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} *[https://www.hyogo-tourism.jp/spot/220 Hyogo Tourism home page]{{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/shiseijoho/shokai/kankouannai/1003058/kankou_tada.html Kawanishi City official website]{{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Settsu Province]] [[Category:Kawanishi, Hyōgo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Tada Shrine
多田神社 | image = Tada-jinja honden.jpg | caption = ''Honden'' of Tada Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hyogo Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|51|40.6|N|135|24|9.4|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Tada Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 978 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Tadain Tadadokorocho, Kawanishi-shi, Hyōgo-ken 666-0127 | website = {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 10 and October 27 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tada Shrine'''|多田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Tadadokoro neighborhood of the city of [[Kawanishi, Hyōgo|Kawanishi]] in [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is the shrine of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan from which [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and many subsequent [[Shogun]] and ''[[daimyō]]'' clans claimed descent. This shrine is one of [[Three Genji Shrines]], with [[Rokusonnō Shrine]] in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]] and [[Tsuboi Hachimangū]] in [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]]. The shrine was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1951.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1877|title=多田神社|trans-title= Tada Jinja|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tada Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]]'''| 源満仲|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Tada Daigongen'''| 多田大権現|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]]'''| 源頼光 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]'''| 源頼信 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]]'''| 源頼義 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriie]]'''| 源義家 |}} ==History== In 970, [[Settsu Province|Settsu-no-kami]] [[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], the founder of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan, received an oracle from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha|Sumiyoshi Ōkami]] to established his residence in what is now Kawanishi. A temple (commonly known as Tada-in) was built, as Mitsunaka, converted to Buddhism following the visit of Ingen (954–1028), [[Genshin]] (942–1017), and Kaku'ub, of the [[Tendai]] sect. The new convert declared, "You have shown me such extraordinary virtuous things. I'm someone who has killed an immeasurable number of living things. I'd like to atone for these sins. By building a Buddhist Hall I will stop committing any more crimes and save the lives of living things." "What is known as Tada-in is a cluster of halls that began to be built with this one."{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |page=22-29}} When Mitsunaka died in 997 he was buried in Tada-in. In the [[Kamakura period]], the temple was regarded as the ancestral mausoleum of Seiwa-Genji, but it gradually declined. In the middle of the Kamakura period, [[Hojo Yasutoki]], the ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura Shogunate]], became the land steward of Tada-sho ''[[shōen]]'', he decided to carry out a large-scale reconstruction of the Tada-in. In 1273, [[Ninsho]] of [[Saidai-ji]] was appointed to undertake the restoration, and as a result, the temple changed from the Tendai sect to the [[Shingon Risshu]] sect. In the [[Muromachi period]], [[Ashikaga Takauji]], who was a descendent of the Seiwa Genji, revered the Tada-in, and temple became a mortuary temple for the [[Ashikaga shogunate]], with the ashes of successive generations of [[shogun]] buried at the temple. However, the temple was destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]] in 1577. It remained in ruins until restored in 1665 by [[Sakakibara Tadatsugu]] of [[Himeji Domain]], [[Inaba Masanori]] of [[Odawara Domain]] and the descendants of the Tada-in retainers. Shogun [[Tokugawa Ietsuna]] granted an estate with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep, and most of its structures were rebuilt by 1667. As with the Ashikaga, successive generation of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa shogun]]s ashes and ''[[ihai]]'' mortuary tablets with enshrined at the temple. Shogun [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] restored the main shrine building in 1695. In the [[Meiji period]], due to the [[shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shintoism and Buddhism]] by the government, Tada-in was transformed into a [[Shinto shrine]] enshrining Minamoto no Mitsunaka, [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]], [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] as ''[[kami]]''. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Gallery== File:Tada-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|Haiden（ICP） File:Tada-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon（ICP） File:Tada-jinja, nandaimon.jpg|MInami Daimon（兵庫県指定文化財） File:Tada-jinja, higashimon.jpg|Higashi-mon（兵庫県指定文化財） File:Tada-jinja, nishimon.jpg|Nishi-mon（兵庫県指定文化財） File:Tada-jinja, otorii.jpg|Ōtorii File:Tada-jinja byo.jpg|Grave of Minamoto no Yorimitsu ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Honden|多田神社 本殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156915|title=多田神社 本殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Haiden|多田神社 拝殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/186526|title=多田神社 拝殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Zuishimon|多田神社 随神門||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/123387|title=多田神社 随神門|trans-title=Tada Jinja Zuishinmonlanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Bunsho|多田神社文書||}}, 43 scrolls with 492 document{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/206080|title=多田神社文書|trans-title=Tada Jinja Bunsholanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ===National Tangible Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Treasury|多田神社宝物殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/442906|title=多田神社宝物殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja homotsuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Tada Shrine}} * {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} *[https://www.hyogo-tourism.jp/spot/220 Hyogo Tourism home page]{{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/shiseijoho/shokai/kankouannai/1003058/kankou_tada.html Kawanishi City official website]{{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Settsu Province]] [[Category:Kawanishi, Hyōgo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]]

|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/123387|title=多田神社 随神門|trans-title=Tada Jinja Zuishinmonlanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Bunsho|多田神社文書||}}, 43 scrolls with 492 document{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/206080|title=多田神社文書|trans-title=Tada Jinja Bunsholanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ===National Tangible Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Treasury|多田神社宝物殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web {{Infobox religious building | name = Tada Shrine
多田神社 | image = Tada-jinja honden.jpg | caption = ''Honden'' of Tada Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hyogo Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|51|40.6|N|135|24|9.4|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Tada Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 978 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Tadain Tadadokorocho, Kawanishi-shi, Hyōgo-ken 666-0127 | website = {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 10 and October 27 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tada Shrine'''|多田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Tadadokoro neighborhood of the city of [[Kawanishi, Hyōgo|Kawanishi]] in [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is the shrine of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan from which [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and many subsequent [[Shogun]] and ''[[daimyō]]'' clans claimed descent. This shrine is one of [[Three Genji Shrines]], with [[Rokusonnō Shrine]] in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]] and [[Tsuboi Hachimangū]] in [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]]. The shrine was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1951.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1877|title=多田神社|trans-title= Tada Jinja|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tada Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]]'''| 源満仲|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Tada Daigongen'''| 多田大権現|}} Minamoto no Mitsunaka, also known as Tada no Manjū, is the oldest son of Minamoto no Tsunemoto. Due to his involvement in exposing the Anna Incident, he established himself to be a formidable samurai and was able to earn many governmental positions in various provinces. After serving as governor of Settsu Province, he helped to train various samurais alongside his retainers. Later on in his last few years, Mitsunaka became a Buddhist monk. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]]'''| 源頼光 |}} Minamoto no Yorimitsu is the eldest son of Mitsunaka. He served the Fujiwara clan and accumulated a great deal of wealth through holding government positions over many provinces. He is most famous for his endeavors with his Four Heavenly Kings in later years, such as the subjugation of Shuten-douji and the extermination of the Tsuchigumo. Yorimitsu inherited his father’s post as governor of Settsu Province and eventually became the founder of the Settsu-Genji. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]'''| 源頼信 |}} Minamoto no Yorinobu is the third son of Mitsunaka. Along with his elder brother Yorimitsu, he served the Fujiwara clan and held the position of Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North. He was noted for having excellent martial prowess, and by suppressing Taira no Tadatsune’s rebellion, he laid the foundation for the Minamoto to advance into the eastern part of the country. In his later years, after serving as the governor of Kawachi Province, he became the founder of the Kawachi-Genji line. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]]'''| 源頼義 |}} Minamoto no Yoriyoshi is the eldest son of Yorinobu, inheriting his father’s excellent skill in martial arts. He gained fame in the eastern part of Japan during his time serving as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a naval base general. He guaranteed the high reputation and prosperity of the Minamoto in the eastern provinces by putting an end to the Early Nine Years’ War. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoshiie]]'''| 源義家 |}} Minamoto no Yoshiie is the eldest son of Yoriyoshi, and he is widely known as “Hachiman-taro” due to his exceptional martial prowess. Following his father’s footsteps, he served as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a military leader in the eastern part of the country. He was able to successfully put an end to the Later Three Years’ War, earning him the title of the “greatest and bravest warrior in the world”. ==History== In 970, [[Settsu Province|Settsu-no-kami]] [[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], the founder of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan, received an oracle from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha|Sumiyoshi Ōkami]] to established his residence in what is now Kawanishi. A temple (commonly known as Tada-in) was built, as Mitsunaka, converted to Buddhism following the visit of Ingen (954–1028), [[Genshin]] (942–1017), and Kaku'ub, of the [[Tendai]] sect. The new convert declared, "You have shown me such extraordinary virtuous things. I'm someone who has killed an immeasurable number of living things. I'd like to atone for these sins. By building a Buddhist Hall I will stop committing any more crimes and save the lives of living things." "What is known as Tada-in is a cluster of halls that began to be built with this one."{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |page=22-29}} When Mitsunaka died in 997 he was buried in Tada-in. In the [[Kamakura period]], the temple was regarded as the ancestral mausoleum of Seiwa-Genji, but it gradually declined. In the middle of the Kamakura period, [[Hojo Yasutoki]], the ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura Shogunate]], became the land steward of Tada-sho ''[[shōen]]'', he decided to carry out a large-scale reconstruction of the Tada-in. In 1273, [[Ninsho]] of [[Saidai-ji]] was appointed to undertake the restoration, and as a result, the temple changed from the Tendai sect to the [[Shingon Risshu]] sect. In the [[Muromachi period]], [[Ashikaga Takauji]], who was a descendent of the Seiwa Genji, revered the Tada-in, and temple became a mortuary temple for the [[Ashikaga shogunate]], with the ashes of successive generations of [[shogun]] buried at the temple. However, the temple was destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]] in 1577. It remained in ruins until restored in 1665 by [[Sakakibara Tadatsugu]] of [[Himeji Domain]], [[Inaba Masanori]] of [[Odawara Domain]] and the descendants of the Tada-in retainers. Shogun [[Tokugawa Ietsuna]] granted an estate with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep, and most of its structures were rebuilt by 1667. As with the Ashikaga, successive generation of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa shogun]]s ashes and ''[[ihai]]'' mortuary tablets with enshrined at the temple. Shogun [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] restored the main shrine building in 1695. In the [[Meiji period]], due to the [[shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shintoism and Buddhism]] by the government, Tada-in was transformed into a [[Shinto shrine]] enshrining Minamoto no Mitsunaka, [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]], [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] as ''[[kami]]''. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Gallery== File:Tada-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, nandaimon.jpg|MInami Daimon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, higashimon.jpg|Higashi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, nishimon.jpg|Nishi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, otorii.jpg|Ōtorii File:Tada-jinja byo.jpg|Grave of Minamoto no Yorimitsu ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Honden|多田神社 本殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156915|title=多田神社 本殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Haiden|多田神社 拝殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/186526|title=多田神社 拝殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Zuishimon|多田神社 随神門||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/123387|title=多田神社 随神門|trans-title=Tada Jinja Zuishinmonlanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Bunsho|多田神社文書||}}, 43 scrolls with 492 document{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/206080|title=多田神社文書|trans-title=Tada Jinja Bunsholanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ===National Tangible Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Treasury|多田神社宝物殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/442906|title=多田神社宝物殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja homotsuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Tada Shrine}} * {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} *[https://www.hyogo-tourism.jp/spot/220 Hyogo Tourism home page]{{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/shiseijoho/shokai/kankouannai/1003058/kankou_tada.html Kawanishi City official website]{{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto Along with his elder brother Yorimitsu, he served the Fujiwara clan and held the position of Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North. He was noted for having excellent martial prowess, and by suppressing Taira no Tadatsune’s rebellion, he laid the foundation for the Minamoto to advance into the eastern part of the country. In his later years, after serving as the governor of Kawachi Province, he became the founder of the Kawachi-Genji line. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]]'''| 源頼義 |}} Minamoto no Yoriyoshi is the eldest son of Yorinobu, inheriting his father’s excellent skill in martial arts. He gained fame in the eastern part of Japan during his time serving as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a naval base general. He guaranteed the high reputation and prosperity of the Minamoto in the eastern provinces by putting an end to the Early Nine Years’ War. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoshiie]]'''| 源義家 |}} Minamoto no Yoshiie is the eldest son of Yoriyoshi, and he is widely known as “Hachiman-taro” due to his exceptional martial prowess. Following his father’s footsteps, he served as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a military leader in the eastern part of the country. He was able to successfully put an end to the Later Three Years’ War, earning him the title of the “greatest and bravest warrior in the world”. ==History== In 970, [[Settsu Province|Settsu-no-kami]] [[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], the founder of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan, received an oracle from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha|Sumiyoshi Ōkami]] to established his residence in what is now Kawanishi. A temple (commonly known as Tada-in) was built, as Mitsunaka, converted to Buddhism following the visit of Ingen (954–1028), [[Genshin]] (942–1017), and Kaku'ub, of the [[Tendai]] sect. The new convert declared, "You have shown me such extraordinary virtuous things. I'm someone who has killed an immeasurable number of living things. I'd like to atone for these sins. By building a Buddhist Hall I will stop committing any more crimes and save the lives of living things." "What is known as Tada-in is a cluster of halls that began to be built with this one."{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |page=22-29}} When Mitsunaka died in 997 he was buried in Tada-in. In the [[Kamakura period]], the temple was regarded as the ancestral mausoleum of Seiwa-Genji, but it gradually declined. In the middle of the Kamakura period, [[Hojo Yasutoki]], the ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura Shogunate]], became the land steward of Tada-sho ''[[shōen]]'', he decided to carry out a large-scale reconstruction of the Tada-in. In 1273, [[Ninsho]] of [[Saidai-ji]] was appointed to undertake the restoration, and as a result, the temple changed from the Tendai sect to the [[Shingon Risshu]] sect. In the [[Muromachi period]], [[Ashikaga Takauji]], who was a descendent of the Seiwa Genji, revered the Tada-in, and temple became a mortuary temple for the [[Ashikaga shogunate]], with the ashes of successive generations of [[shogun]] buried at the temple. However, the temple was destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]] in 1577. It remained in ruins until restored in 1665 by [[Sakakibara Tadatsugu]] of [[Himeji Domain]], [[Inaba Masanori]] of [[Odawara Domain]] and the descendants of the Tada-in retainers. Shogun [[Tokugawa Ietsuna]] granted an estate with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep, and most of its structures were rebuilt by 1667. As with the Ashikaga, successive generation of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa shogun]]s ashes and ''[[ihai]]'' mortuary tablets with enshrined at the temple. Shogun [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] restored the main shrine building in 1695. In the [[Meiji period]], due to the [[shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shintoism and Buddhism]] by the government, Tada-in was transformed into a [[Shinto shrine]] enshrining Minamoto no Mitsunaka, [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]], [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] as ''[[kami]]''. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Gallery== File:Tada-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, nandaimon.jpg|MInami Daimon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, higashimon.jpg|Higashi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, nishimon.jpg|Nishi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, otorii.jpg|Ōtorii File:Tada-jinja byo.jpg|Grave of Minamoto no Yorimitsu ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Honden|多田神社 本殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156915|title=多田神社 本殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Haiden|多田神社 拝殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/186526|title=多田神社 拝殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Zuishimon|多田神社 随神門||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/123387|title=多田神社 随神門|trans-title=Tada Jinja Zuishinmonlanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Bunsho|多田神社文書||}}, 43 scrolls with 492 document{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/206080|title=多田神社文書|trans-title=Tada Jinja Bunsholanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ===National Tangible Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Treasury|多田神社宝物殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/442906|title=多田神社宝物殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja homotsuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Tada Shrine}} * *[https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/shiseijoho/shokai/kankouannai/1003058/kankou_tada.html Kawanishi City official website]{{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto {{Infobox religious building | name = Tada Shrine
多田神社 | image = Tada-jinja honden.jpg | caption = ''Honden'' of Tada Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hyogo Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|51|40.6|N|135|24|9.4|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Tada Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 978 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Tadain Tadadokorocho, Kawanishi-shi, Hyōgo-ken 666-0127 | website = {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 10 and October 27 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tada Shrine'''|多田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Tadadokoro neighborhood of the city of [[Kawanishi, Hyōgo|Kawanishi]] in [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is the shrine of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan from which [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and many subsequent [[Shogun]] and ''[[daimyō]]'' clans claimed descent. This shrine is one of [[Three Genji Shrines]], with [[Rokusonnō Shrine]] in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]] and [[Tsuboi Hachimangū]] in [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]]. The shrine was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1951.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1877|title=多田神社|trans-title= Tada Jinja|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tada Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]]'''| 源満仲|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Tada Daigongen'''| 多田大権現|}} Minamoto no Mitsunaka, also known as Tada no Manjū, is the oldest son of Minamoto no Tsunemoto. Due to his involvement in exposing the Anna Incident, he established himself to be a formidable samurai and was able to earn many governmental positions in various provinces. After serving as governor of Settsu Province, he helped to train various samurais alongside his retainers. Later on in his last few years, Mitsunaka became a Buddhist monk. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]]'''| 源頼光 |}} Minamoto no Yorimitsu is the eldest son of Mitsunaka. He served the Fujiwara clan and accumulated a great deal of wealth through holding government positions over many provinces. He is most famous for his endeavors with his Four Heavenly Kings in later years, such as the subjugation of Shuten-douji and the extermination of the Tsuchigumo. Yorimitsu inherited his father’s post as governor of Settsu Province and eventually became the founder of the Settsu-Genji. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]'''| 源頼信 |}} Minamoto no Yorinobu is the third son of Mitsunaka. Along with his elder brother Yorimitsu, he served the Fujiwara clan and held the position of Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North. He was noted for having excellent martial prowess, and by suppressing Taira no Tadatsune’s rebellion, he laid the foundation for the Minamoto to advance into the eastern part of the country. In his later years, after serving as the governor of Kawachi Province, he became the founder of the Kawachi-Genji line. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]]'''| 源頼義 |}} Minamoto no Yoriyoshi is the eldest son of Yorinobu, inheriting his father’s excellent skill in martial arts. He gained fame in the eastern part of Japan during his time serving as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a naval base general. He guaranteed the high reputation and prosperity of the Minamoto in the eastern provinces by putting an end to the Early Nine Years’ War. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoshiie]]'''| 源義家 |}} Minamoto no Yoshiie is the eldest son of Yoriyoshi, and he is widely known as “Hachiman-taro” due to his exceptional martial prowess. Following his father’s footsteps, he served as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a military leader in the eastern part of the country. He was able to successfully put an end to the Later Three Years’ War, earning him the title of the “greatest and bravest warrior in the world”. ==History== In 970, [[Settsu Province|Settsu-no-kami]] [[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], the founder of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan, received an oracle from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha|Sumiyoshi Ōkami]] to established his residence in what is now Kawanishi. A temple (commonly known as Tada-in) was built, as Mitsunaka, converted to Buddhism following the visit of Ingen (954–1028), [[Genshin]] (942–1017), and Kaku'ub, of the [[Tendai]] sect. The new convert declared, "You have shown me such extraordinary virtuous things. I'm someone who has killed an immeasurable number of living things. I'd like to atone for these sins. By building a Buddhist Hall I will stop committing any more crimes and save the lives of living things." "What is known as Tada-in is a cluster of halls that began to be built with this one."{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |page=22-29}} When Mitsunaka died in 997 he was buried in Tada-in. In the [[Kamakura period]], the temple was regarded as the ancestral mausoleum of Seiwa-Genji, but it gradually declined. In the middle of the Kamakura period, [[Hojo Yasutoki]], the ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura Shogunate]], became the land steward of Tada-sho ''[[shōen]]'', he decided to carry out a large-scale reconstruction of the Tada-in. In 1273, [[Ninsho]] of [[Saidai-ji]] was appointed to undertake the restoration, and as a result, the temple changed from the Tendai sect to the [[Shingon Risshu]] sect. In the [[Muromachi period]], [[Ashikaga Takauji]], who was a descendent of the Seiwa Genji, revered the Tada-in, and temple became a mortuary temple for the [[Ashikaga shogunate]], with the ashes of successive generations of [[shogun]] buried at the temple. However, the temple was destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]] in 1577. It remained in ruins until restored in 1665 by [[Sakakibara Tadatsugu]] of [[Himeji Domain]], [[Inaba Masanori]] of [[Odawara Domain]] and the descendants of the Tada-in retainers. Shogun [[Tokugawa Ietsuna]] granted an estate with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep, and most of its structures were rebuilt by 1667. As with the Ashikaga, successive generation of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa shogun]]s ashes and ''[[ihai]]'' mortuary tablets with enshrined at the temple. Shogun [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] restored the main shrine building in 1695. In the [[Meiji period]], due to the [[shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shintoism and Buddhism]] by the government, Tada-in was transformed into a [[Shinto shrine]] enshrining Minamoto no Mitsunaka, [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]], [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] as ''[[kami]]''. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Gallery== File:Tada-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, nandaimon.jpg|MInami Daimon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, higashimon.jpg|Higashi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, nishimon.jpg|Nishi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, otorii.jpg|Ōtorii File:Tada-jinja byo.jpg|Grave of Minamoto no Yorimitsu ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Honden|多田神社 本殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156915|title=多田神社 本殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Haiden|多田神社 拝殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/186526|title=多田神社 拝殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Zuishimon|多田神社 随神門||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/123387|title=多田神社 随神門|trans-title=Tada Jinja Zuishinmonlanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Bunsho|多田神社文書||}}, 43 scrolls with 492 document{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/206080|title=多田神社文書|trans-title=Tada Jinja Bunsholanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ===National Tangible Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Treasury|多田神社宝物殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/442906|title=多田神社宝物殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja homotsuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ==See |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ===National Tangible Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Treasury|多田神社宝物殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Commons category link is defined as the pagename]] to [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:FULLPAGENAME}}]][[category:dd]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Tada Shrine
多田神社 | image = Tada-jinja honden.jpg | caption = ''Honden'' of Tada Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hyogo Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|51|40.6|N|135|24|9.4|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Tada Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 978 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Tadain Tadadokorocho, Kawanishi-shi, Hyōgo-ken 666-0127 | website = {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 10 and October 27 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tada Shrine'''|多田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Tadadokoro neighborhood of the city of [[Kawanishi, Hyōgo|Kawanishi]] in [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is the shrine of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan from which [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and many subsequent [[Shogun]] and ''[[daimyō]]'' clans claimed descent. This shrine is one of [[Three Genji Shrines]], with [[Rokusonnō Shrine]] in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]] and [[Tsuboi Hachimangū]] in [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]]. The shrine was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1951.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1877|title=多田神社|trans-title= Tada Jinja|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tada Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]]'''| 源満仲|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Tada Daigongen'''| 多田大権現|}} Minamoto no Mitsunaka, also known as Tada no Manjū, is the oldest son of Minamoto no Tsunemoto. Due to his involvement in exposing the Anna Incident, he established himself to be a formidable samurai and was able to earn many governmental positions in various provinces. After serving as governor of Settsu Province, he helped to train various samurais alongside his retainers. Later on in his last few years, Mitsunaka became a Buddhist monk. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]]'''| 源頼光 |}} Minamoto no Yorimitsu is the eldest son of Mitsunaka. He served the Fujiwara clan and accumulated a great deal of wealth through holding government positions over many provinces. He is most famous for his endeavors with his Four Heavenly Kings in later years, such as the subjugation of Shuten-douji and the extermination of the Tsuchigumo. Yorimitsu inherited his father’s post as governor of Settsu Province and eventually became the founder of the Settsu-Genji. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]'''| 源頼信 |}} Minamoto no Yorinobu is the third son of Mitsunaka. Along with his elder brother Yorimitsu, he served the Fujiwara clan and held the position of Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North. He was noted for having excellent martial prowess, and by suppressing Taira no Tadatsune’s rebellion, he laid the foundation for the Minamoto to advance into the eastern part of the country. In his later years, after serving as the governor of Kawachi Province, he became the founder of the Kawachi-Genji line. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]]'''| 源頼義 |}} Minamoto no Yoriyoshi is the eldest son of Yorinobu, inheriting his father’s excellent skill in martial arts. He gained fame in the eastern part of Japan during his time serving as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a naval base general. He guaranteed the high reputation and prosperity of the Minamoto in the eastern provinces by putting an end to the Early Nine Years’ War. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoshiie]]'''| 源義家 |}} Minamoto no Yoshiie is the eldest son of Yoriyoshi, and he is widely known as “Hachiman-taro” due to his exceptional martial prowess. Following his father’s footsteps, he served as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a military leader in the eastern part of the country. He was able to successfully put an end to the Later Three Years’ War, earning him the title of the “greatest and bravest warrior in the world”. ==History== In 970, [[Settsu Province|Settsu-no-kami]] [[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], the founder of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan, received an oracle from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha|Sumiyoshi Ōkami]] to established his residence in what is now Kawanishi. A temple (commonly known as Tada-in) was built, as Mitsunaka, converted to Buddhism following the visit of Ingen (954–1028), [[Genshin]] (942–1017), and Kaku'ub, of the [[Tendai]] sect. The new convert declared, "You have shown me such extraordinary virtuous things. I'm someone who has killed an immeasurable number of living things. I'd like to atone for these sins. By building a Buddhist Hall I will stop committing any more crimes and save the lives of living things." "What is known as Tada-in is a cluster of halls that began to be built with this one."{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |page=22-29}} When Mitsunaka died in 997 he was buried in Tada-in. In the [[Kamakura period]], the temple was regarded as the ancestral mausoleum of Seiwa-Genji, but it gradually declined. In the middle of the Kamakura period, [[Hojo Yasutoki]], the ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura Shogunate]], became the land steward of Tada-sho ''[[shōen]]'', he decided to carry out a large-scale reconstruction of the Tada-in. In 1273, [[Ninsho]] of [[Saidai-ji]] was appointed to undertake the restoration, and as a result, the temple changed from the Tendai sect to the [[Shingon Risshu]] sect. In the [[Muromachi period]], [[Ashikaga Takauji]], who was a descendent of the Seiwa Genji, revered the Tada-in, and temple became a mortuary temple for the [[Ashikaga shogunate]], with the ashes of successive generations of [[shogun]] buried at the temple. However, the temple was destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]] in 1577. It remained in ruins until restored in 1665 by [[Sakakibara Tadatsugu]] of [[Himeji Domain]], [[Inaba Masanori]] of [[Odawara Domain]] and the descendants of the Tada-in retainers. Shogun [[Tokugawa Ietsuna]] granted an estate with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep, and most of its structures were rebuilt by 1667. As with the Ashikaga, successive generation of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa shogun]]s ashes and ''[[ihai]]'' mortuary tablets with enshrined at the temple. Shogun [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] restored the main shrine building in 1695. In the [[Meiji period]], due to the [[shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shintoism and Buddhism]] by the government, Tada-in was transformed into a [[Shinto shrine]] enshrining Minamoto no Mitsunaka, [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]], [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] as ''[[kami]]''. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Gallery== File:Tada-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, nandaimon.jpg|MInami Daimon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, higashimon.jpg|Higashi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, nishimon.jpg|Nishi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, otorii.jpg|Ōtorii File:Tada-jinja byo.jpg|Grave of Minamoto no Yorimitsu ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Honden|多田神社 本殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156915|title=多田神社 本殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Haiden|多田神社 拝殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/186526|title=多田神社 拝殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Zuishimon|多田神社 随神門||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/123387|title=多田神社 随神門|trans-title=Tada Jinja Zuishinmonlanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Bunsho|多田神社文書||}}, 43 scrolls with 492 document{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/206080|title=多田神社文書|trans-title=Tada Jinja Bunsholanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ===National Tangible Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Treasury|多田神社宝物殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/442906|title=多田神社宝物殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja homotsuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Tada Shrine}} * {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} *[https://www.hyogo-tourism.jp/spot/220 Hyogo Tourism home page]{{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/shiseijoho/shokai/kankouannai/1003058/kankou_tada.html Kawanishi City official website]{{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Settsu Province]] [[Category:Kawanishi, Hyōgo]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Tada Shrine]][[Category:Hyogo Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:Tada Shrine]][[category:dd]]

He served the Fujiwara clan and accumulated a great deal of wealth through holding government positions over many provinces. He is most famous for his endeavors with his Four Heavenly Kings in later years, such as the subjugation of Shuten-douji and the extermination of the Tsuchigumo. Yorimitsu inherited his father’s post as governor of Settsu Province and eventually became the founder of the Settsu-Genji. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]'''| 源頼信 |}} Minamoto no Yorinobu is the third son of Mitsunaka. Along with his elder brother Yorimitsu, he served the Fujiwara clan and held the position of Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North. He was noted for having excellent martial prowess, and by suppressing Taira no Tadatsune’s rebellion, he laid the foundation for the Minamoto to advance into the eastern part of the country. In his later years, after serving as the governor of Kawachi Province, he became the founder of the Kawachi-Genji line. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]]'''| 源頼義 |}} Minamoto no Yoriyoshi is the eldest son of Yorinobu, inheriting his father’s excellent skill in martial arts. He gained fame in the eastern part of Japan during his time serving as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a naval base general. He guaranteed the high reputation and prosperity of the Minamoto in the eastern provinces by putting an end to the Early Nine Years’ War. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoshiie]]'''| 源義家 |}} Minamoto no Yoshiie is the eldest son of Yoriyoshi, and he is widely known as “Hachiman-taro” due to his exceptional martial prowess. Following his father’s footsteps, he served as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a military leader in the eastern part of the country. He was able to successfully put an end to the Later Three Years’ War, earning him the title of the “greatest and bravest warrior in the world”. ==History== In 970, [[Settsu Province|Settsu-no-kami]] [[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], the founder of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan, received an oracle from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha|Sumiyoshi Ōkami]] to established his residence in what is now Kawanishi. A temple (commonly known as Tada-in) was built, as Mitsunaka, converted to Buddhism following the visit of Ingen (954–1028), [[Genshin]] (942–1017), and Kaku'ub, of the [[Tendai]] sect. The new convert declared, "You have shown me such extraordinary virtuous things. I'm someone who has killed an immeasurable number of living things. I'd like to atone for these sins. By building a Buddhist Hall I will stop committing any more crimes and save the lives of living things." "What is known as Tada-in is a cluster of halls that began to be built with this one."{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |page=22-29}} When Mitsunaka died in 997 he was buried in Tada-in. In the [[Kamakura period]], the temple was regarded as the ancestral mausoleum of Seiwa-Genji, but it gradually declined. In the middle of the Kamakura period, [[Hojo Yasutoki]], the ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura Shogunate]], became the land steward of Tada-sho ''[[shōen]]'', he decided to carry out a large-scale reconstruction of the Tada-in. In 1273, [[Ninsho]] of [[Saidai-ji]] was appointed to undertake the restoration, and as a result, the temple changed from the Tendai sect to the [[Shingon Risshu]] sect. In the [[Muromachi period]], [[Ashikaga Takauji]], who was a descendent of the Seiwa Genji, revered the Tada-in, and temple became a mortuary temple for the [[Ashikaga shogunate]], with the ashes of successive generations of [[shogun]] buried at the temple. However, the temple was destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]] in 1577. It remained in ruins until restored in 1665 by [[Sakakibara Tadatsugu]] of [[Himeji Domain]], [[Inaba Masanori]] of [[Odawara Domain]] and the descendants of the Tada-in retainers. Shogun [[Tokugawa Ietsuna]] granted an estate with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep, and most of its structures were rebuilt by 1667. As with the Ashikaga, successive generation of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa shogun]]s ashes and ''[[ihai]]'' mortuary tablets with enshrined at the temple. Shogun [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] restored the main shrine building in 1695. In the [[Meiji period]], due to the [[shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shintoism and Buddhism]] by the government, Tada-in was transformed into a [[Shinto shrine]] enshrining Minamoto no Mitsunaka, [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]], [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] as ''[[kami]]''. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Gallery== File:Tada-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, nandaimon.jpg|MInami Daimon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, higashimon.jpg|Higashi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, nishimon.jpg|Nishi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, otorii.jpg|Ōtorii File:Tada-jinja byo.jpg|Grave of Minamoto no Yorimitsu ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Honden|多田神社 本殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156915|title=多田神社 本殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Haiden|多田神社 拝殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/186526|title=多田神社 拝殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Zuishimon|多田神社 随神門||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/123387|title=多田神社 随神門|trans-title=Tada Jinja Zuishinmonlanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Bunsho|多田神社文書||}}, 43 scrolls with 492 document{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/206080|title=多田神社文書|trans-title=Tada Jinja Bunsholanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ===National Tangible Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Treasury|多田神社宝物殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/442906|title=多田神社宝物殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja homotsuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Tada Shrine}} * {{Infobox religious building | name = Tada Shrine
多田神社 | image = Tada-jinja honden.jpg | caption = ''Honden'' of Tada Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hyogo Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|51|40.6|N|135|24|9.4|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title, inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Tada Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = 978 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Tadain Tadadokorocho, Kawanishi-shi, Hyōgo-ken 666-0127 | website = {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 10 and October 27 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tada Shrine'''|多田神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Tadadokoro neighborhood of the city of [[Kawanishi, Hyōgo|Kawanishi]] in [[Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is the shrine of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan from which [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and many subsequent [[Shogun]] and ''[[daimyō]]'' clans claimed descent. This shrine is one of [[Three Genji Shrines]], with [[Rokusonnō Shrine]] in [[Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto]] and [[Tsuboi Hachimangū]] in [[Osaka Prefecture|Osaka]]. The shrine was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1951.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1877|title=多田神社|trans-title= Tada Jinja|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tada Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]]'''| 源満仲|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Tada Daigongen'''| 多田大権現|}} Minamoto no Mitsunaka, also known as Tada no Manjū, is the oldest son of Minamoto no Tsunemoto. Due to his involvement in exposing the Anna Incident, he established himself to be a formidable samurai and was able to earn many governmental positions in various provinces. After serving as governor of Settsu Province, he helped to train various samurais alongside his retainers. Later on in his last few years, Mitsunaka became a Buddhist monk. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]]'''| 源頼光 |}} Minamoto no Yorimitsu is the eldest son of Mitsunaka. He served the Fujiwara clan and accumulated a great deal of wealth through holding government positions over many provinces. He is most famous for his endeavors with his Four Heavenly Kings in later years, such as the subjugation of Shuten-douji and the extermination of the Tsuchigumo. Yorimitsu inherited his father’s post as governor of Settsu Province and eventually became the founder of the Settsu-Genji. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]'''| 源頼信 |}} Minamoto no Yorinobu is the third son of Mitsunaka. Along with his elder brother Yorimitsu, he served the Fujiwara clan and held the position of Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North. He was noted for having excellent martial prowess, and by suppressing Taira no Tadatsune’s rebellion, he laid the foundation for the Minamoto to advance into the eastern part of the country. In his later years, after serving as the governor of Kawachi Province, he became the founder of the Kawachi-Genji line. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]]'''| 源頼義 |}} Minamoto no Yoriyoshi is the eldest son of Yorinobu, inheriting his father’s excellent skill in martial arts. He gained fame in the eastern part of Japan during his time serving as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a naval base general. He guaranteed the high reputation and prosperity of the Minamoto in the eastern provinces by putting an end to the Early Nine Years’ War. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoshiie]]'''| 源義家 |}} Minamoto no Yoshiie is the eldest son of Yoriyoshi, and he is widely known as “Hachiman-taro” due to his exceptional martial prowess. Following his father’s footsteps, he served as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a military leader in the eastern part of the country. He was able to successfully put an end to the Later Three Years’ War, earning him the title of the “greatest and bravest warrior in the world”. ==History== In 970, [[Settsu Province|Settsu-no-kami]] [[Minamoto no Mitsunaka]], the founder of the [[Seiwa Genji]] clan, received an oracle from [[Sumiyoshi Taisha|Sumiyoshi Ōkami]] to established his residence in what is now Kawanishi. A temple (commonly known as Tada-in) was built, as Mitsunaka, converted to Buddhism following the visit of Ingen (954–1028), [[Genshin]] (942–1017), and Kaku'ub, of the [[Tendai]] sect. The new convert declared, "You have shown me such extraordinary virtuous things. I'm someone who has killed an immeasurable number of living things. I'd like to atone for these sins. By building a Buddhist Hall I will stop committing any more crimes and save the lives of living things." "What is known as Tada-in is a cluster of halls that began to be built with this one."{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |page=22-29}} When Mitsunaka died in 997 he was buried in Tada-in. In the [[Kamakura period]], the temple was regarded as the ancestral mausoleum of Seiwa-Genji, but it gradually declined. In the middle of the Kamakura period, [[Hojo Yasutoki]], the ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura Shogunate]], became the land steward of Tada-sho ''[[shōen]]'', he decided to carry out a large-scale reconstruction of the Tada-in. In 1273, [[Ninsho]] of [[Saidai-ji]] was appointed to undertake the restoration, and as a result, the temple changed from the Tendai sect to the [[Shingon Risshu]] sect. In the [[Muromachi period]], [[Ashikaga Takauji]], who was a descendent of the Seiwa Genji, revered the Tada-in, and temple became a mortuary temple for the [[Ashikaga shogunate]], with the ashes of successive generations of [[shogun]] buried at the temple. However, the temple was destroyed by the forces of [[Oda Nobunaga]] in 1577. It remained in ruins until restored in 1665 by [[Sakakibara Tadatsugu]] of [[Himeji Domain]], [[Inaba Masanori]] of [[Odawara Domain]] and the descendants of the Tada-in retainers. Shogun [[Tokugawa Ietsuna]] granted an estate with a ''[[kokudaka]]'' of 500 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep, and most of its structures were rebuilt by 1667. As with the Ashikaga, successive generation of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa shogun]]s ashes and ''[[ihai]]'' mortuary tablets with enshrined at the temple. Shogun [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] restored the main shrine building in 1695. In the [[Meiji period]], due to the [[shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shintoism and Buddhism]] by the government, Tada-in was transformed into a [[Shinto shrine]] enshrining Minamoto no Mitsunaka, [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]], [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] as ''[[kami]]''. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Gallery== File:Tada-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, nandaimon.jpg|MInami Daimon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, higashimon.jpg|Higashi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, nishimon.jpg|Nishi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, otorii.jpg|Ōtorii File:Tada-jinja byo.jpg|Grave of Minamoto no Yorimitsu ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Honden|多田神社 本殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156915|title=多田神社 本殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Haiden|多田神社 拝殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/186526|title=多田神社 拝殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Zuishimon|多田神社 随神門||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/123387|title=多田神社 随神門|trans-title=Tada Jinja Zuishinmonlanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Bunsho|多田神社文書||}}, 43 scrolls with 492 document{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/206080|title=多田神社文書|trans-title=Tada Jinja Bunsholanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ===National Tangible Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Treasury|多田神社宝物殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/442906|title=多田神社宝物殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja homotsuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ==See
