revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Tada-jinja]][[en:Tada Shrine]][[fr:Tada-jinja]][[ja:多田神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Tada-jinja]][[en:Tada Shrine]][[fr:Tada-jinja]][[ja:多田神社]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Tada-jinja]][[en:Tada Shrine]][[fr:Tada-jinja]][[ja:多田神社]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Tada Shrine
多田神社 | image = File:Tada Jinja.jpg | caption = ''Honden'' of Tada Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hyōgo Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.86127778|N|135.402625|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Tada Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|WD=Q2531241|1=Minamoto no Mitsunaka}}; {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|1=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}}; {{ill|Minamoto no Yorinobu|WD=Q2699951|1=Minamoto no Yorinobu}}; {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|WD=Q3082851|1=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}}; {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} | founded_by = | established = 978 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Tadain Tadadokorocho, Kawanishi-shi, Hyōgo-ken 666-0127 | website = {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 10 and October 27 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Kawanishi|WD=Q609350|1=Kawanishi, Hyōgo}} }} {{nihongo|'''Tada Shrine'''|多田神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Tadadokoro neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|en|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|WD=Q609350|lt=Kawanishi|1=Kawanishi, Hyōgo}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is the shrine of the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}} clan from which [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and many subsequent {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}} and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' clans claimed descent. This shrine is one of {{ill|Three Genji Shrines|en|Three Genji Shrines|WD=Q7797495|lt=Three Genji Shrines|1=Three Genji Shrines}}, with {{ill|Rokusonnō Shrine|en|Rokusonnō Shrine|WD=Q11392320|lt=Rokusonnō Shrine|1=Rokusonnō Shrine}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} and {{ill|Tsuboi Hachimangū|en|Tsuboi Hachimangū|WD=Q11429677|lt=Tsuboi Hachimangū|1=Tsuboi Hachimangū}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. The shrine was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1951.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1877|title=多田神社|trans-title= Tada Jinja|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tada Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|en|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|WD=Q2531241|lt=Minamoto no Mitsunaka|1=Minamoto no Mitsunaka}}'''| 源満仲|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Tada Daigongen'''| 多田大権現|}} Minamoto no Mitsunaka, also known as Tada no Manjū, is the oldest son of Minamoto no Tsunemoto. Due to his involvement in exposing the Anna Incident, he established himself to be a formidable samurai and was able to earn many governmental positions in various provinces. After serving as governor of Settsu Province, he helped to train various samurais alongside his retainers. Later on in his last few years, Mitsunaka became a Buddhist monk. * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}}'''| 源頼光 |}} Minamoto no Yorimitsu is the eldest son of Mitsunaka. He served the Fujiwara clan and accumulated a great deal of wealth through holding government positions over many provinces. He is most famous for his endeavors with his Four Heavenly Kings in later years, such as the subjugation of Shuten-douji and the extermination of the Tsuchigumo. Yorimitsu inherited his father’s post as governor of Settsu Province and eventually became the founder of the Settsu-Genji. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]'''| 源頼信 |}} Minamoto no Yorinobu is the third son of Mitsunaka. Along with his elder brother Yorimitsu, he served the Fujiwara clan and held the position of Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North. He was noted for having excellent martial prowess, and by suppressing Taira no Tadatsune’s rebellion, he laid the foundation for the Minamoto to advance into the eastern part of the country. In his later years, after serving as the governor of Kawachi Province, he became the founder of the Kawachi-Genji line. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]]'''| 源頼義 |}} Minamoto no Yoriyoshi is the eldest son of Yorinobu, inheriting his father’s excellent skill in martial arts. He gained fame in the eastern part of Japan during his time serving as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a naval base general. He guaranteed the high reputation and prosperity of the Minamoto in the eastern provinces by putting an end to the Early Nine Years’ War. * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}}'''| 源義家 |}} Minamoto no Yoshiie is the eldest son of Yoriyoshi, and he is widely known as “Hachiman-taro” due to his exceptional martial prowess. Following his father’s footsteps, he served as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a military leader in the eastern part of the country. He was able to successfully put an end to the Later Three Years’ War, earning him the title of the “greatest and bravest warrior in the world”. ==History== In 970, {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu-no-kami|1=Settsu Province}} {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|en|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|WD=Q2531241|lt=Minamoto no Mitsunaka|1=Minamoto no Mitsunaka}}, the founder of the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}} clan, received an oracle from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Ōkami]] to established his residence in what is now Kawanishi. A temple (commonly known as Tada-in) was built, as Mitsunaka, converted to Buddhism following the visit of Ingen (954–1028), {{ill|Genshin|en|Genshin|WD=Q1009263|lt=Genshin|1=Genshin}} (942–1017), and Kaku'ub, of the {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} sect. The new convert declared, "You have shown me such extraordinary virtuous things. I'm someone who has killed an immeasurable number of living things. I'd like to atone for these sins. By building a Buddhist Hall I will stop committing any more crimes and save the lives of living things." "What is known as Tada-in is a cluster of halls that began to be built with this one."{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |page=22-29}} When Mitsunaka died in 997 he was buried in Tada-in. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the temple was regarded as the ancestral mausoleum of Seiwa-Genji, but it gradually declined. In the middle of the Kamakura period, [[:en:Hojo Yasutoki|Hojo Yasutoki]], the ''{{ill|Shikken|en|Shikken|WD=Q1045904|lt=Shikken|1=Shikken}}'' of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, became the land steward of Tada-sho ''[[shōen]]'', he decided to carry out a large-scale reconstruction of the Tada-in. In 1273, [[:en:Ninsho|Ninsho]] of [[Saidai-ji]] was appointed to undertake the restoration, and as a result, the temple changed from the Tendai sect to the {{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Risshu|1=Shingon Risshu}} sect. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, who was a descendent of the Seiwa Genji, revered the Tada-in, and temple became a mortuary temple for the {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate}}, with the ashes of successive generations of {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}} buried at the temple. However, the temple was destroyed by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} in 1577. It remained in ruins until restored in 1665 by [[Sakakibara Tadatsugu]] of {{ill|Himeji Domain|en|Himeji Domain|WD=Q1062190|lt=Himeji Domain|1=Himeji Domain}}, {{ill|Inaba Masanori|en|Inaba Masanori|WD=Q6013288|lt=Inaba Masanori|1=Inaba Masanori}} of {{ill|Odawara Domain|en|Odawara Domain|WD=Q903467|lt=Odawara Domain|1=Odawara Domain}} and the descendants of the Tada-in retainers. Shogun {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsuna|en|Tokugawa Ietsuna|WD=Q323422|lt=Tokugawa Ietsuna|1=Tokugawa Ietsuna}} granted an estate with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' of 500 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'' for its upkeep, and most of its structures were rebuilt by 1667. As with the Ashikaga, successive generation of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa shogun]]s ashes and ''[[:en:Ihai|Ihai]]'' mortuary tablets with enshrined at the temple. Shogun [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] restored the main shrine building in 1695. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, due to the [[shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shintoism and Buddhism]] by the government, Tada-in was transformed into a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} enshrining Minamoto no Mitsunaka, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}}, [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} as ''[[kami]]''. The shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Gallery== File:Tada-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, nandaimon.jpg|MInami Daimon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, higashimon.jpg|Higashi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, nishimon.jpg|Nishi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, otorii.jpg|Ōtorii File:Tada-jinja byo.jpg|Grave of Minamoto no Yorimitsu ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Honden|多田神社 本殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156915|title=多田神社 本殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Haiden|多田神社 拝殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/186526|title=多田神社 拝殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Zuishimon|多田神社 随神門||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/123387|title=多田神社 随神門|trans-title=Tada Jinja Zuishinmonlanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Bunsho|多田神社文書||}}, 43 scrolls with 492 document{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/206080|title=多田神社文書|trans-title=Tada Jinja Bunsholanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ===National Tangible Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Treasury|多田神社宝物殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/442906|title=多田神社宝物殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja homotsuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)|WD=Q104629006|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Tada Shrine}} * {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} *[https://www.hyogo-tourism.jp/spot/220 Hyōgo Tourism home page]{{in lang|ja}} *[https://www.city.kawanishi.hyogo.jp/shiseijoho/shokai/kankouannai/1003058/kankou_tada.html Kawanishi City official website]{{in lang|ja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Tada Shrine]] {{Commons category|Tada-jinja}} [[fr:Tada-jinja]] [[ja:多田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q3513683}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] The shrine was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1951.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1877|title=多田神社|trans-title= Tada Jinja|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tada Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|en|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|WD=Q2531241|lt=Minamoto no Mitsunaka|1=Minamoto no Mitsunaka}}'''| 源満仲|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Tada Daigongen'''| 多田大権現|}} Minamoto no Mitsunaka, also known as Tada no Manjū, is the oldest son of Minamoto no Tsunemoto. After serving as governor of Settsu Province, he helped to train various samurais alongside his retainers. Later on in his last few years, Mitsunaka became a Buddhist monk. * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}}'''| 源頼光 |}} Minamoto no Yorimitsu is the eldest son of Mitsunaka. He is most famous for his endeavors with his Four Heavenly Kings in later years, such as the subjugation of Shuten-douji and the extermination of the Tsuchigumo. Along with his elder brother Yorimitsu, he served the Fujiwara clan and held the position of Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North. He was noted for having excellent martial prowess, and by suppressing Taira no Tadatsune’s rebellion, he laid the foundation for the Minamoto to advance into the eastern part of the country. In his later years, after serving as the governor of Kawachi Province, he became the founder of the Kawachi-Genji line. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]]'''| 源頼義 |}} Minamoto no Yoriyoshi is the eldest son of Yorinobu, inheriting his father’s excellent skill in martial arts. He gained fame in the eastern part of Japan during his time serving as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a naval base general. He guaranteed the high reputation and prosperity of the Minamoto in the eastern provinces by putting an end to the Early Nine Years’ War. * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}}'''| 源義家 |}} Minamoto no Yoshiie is the eldest son of Yoriyoshi, and he is widely known as “Hachiman-taro” due to his exceptional martial prowess. Following his father’s footsteps, he served as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a military leader in the eastern part of the country. He was able to successfully put an end to the Later Three Years’ War, earning him the title of the “greatest and bravest warrior in the world”. ==History== In 970, {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu-no-kami|1=Settsu Province}} {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|en|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|WD=Q2531241|lt=Minamoto no Mitsunaka|1=Minamoto no Mitsunaka}}, the founder of the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}} clan, received an oracle from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Ōkami]] to established his residence in what is now Kawanishi. The new convert declared, "You have shown me such extraordinary virtuous things. I'm someone who has killed an immeasurable number of living things. I'd like to atone for these sins. By building a Buddhist Hall I will stop committing any more crimes and save the lives of living things." "What is known as Tada-in is a cluster of halls that began to be built with this one."{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |page=22-29}} When Mitsunaka died in 997 he was buried in Tada-in. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the temple was regarded as the ancestral mausoleum of Seiwa-Genji, but it gradually declined. In the middle of the Kamakura period, [[:en:Hojo Yasutoki|Hojo Yasutoki]], the ''{{ill|Shikken|en|Shikken|WD=Q1045904|lt=Shikken|1=Shikken}}'' of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, became the land steward of Tada-sho ''[[shōen]]'', he decided to carry out a large-scale reconstruction of the Tada-in. In 1273, [[:en:Ninsho|Ninsho]] of [[Saidai-ji]] was appointed to undertake the restoration, and as a result, the temple changed from the Tendai sect to the {{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Risshu|1=Shingon Risshu}} sect. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, who was a descendent of the Seiwa Genji, revered the Tada-in, and temple became a mortuary temple for the {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate}}, with the ashes of successive generations of {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}} buried at the temple. However, the temple was destroyed by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} in 1577. It remained in ruins until restored in 1665 by [[Sakakibara Tadatsugu]] of {{ill|Himeji Domain|en|Himeji Domain|WD=Q1062190|lt=Himeji Domain|1=Himeji Domain}}, {{ill|Inaba Masanori|en|Inaba Masanori|WD=Q6013288|lt=Inaba Masanori|1=Inaba Masanori}} of {{ill|Odawara Domain|en|Odawara Domain|WD=Q903467|lt=Odawara Domain|1=Odawara Domain}} and the descendants of the Tada-in retainers. Shogun {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsuna|en|Tokugawa Ietsuna|WD=Q323422|lt=Tokugawa Ietsuna|1=Tokugawa Ietsuna}} granted an estate with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' of 500 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'' for its upkeep, and most of its structures were rebuilt by 1667. As with the Ashikaga, successive generation of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa shogun]]s ashes and ''[[:en:Ihai|Ihai]]'' mortuary tablets with enshrined at the temple. Shogun [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] restored the main shrine building in 1695. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, due to the [[shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shintoism and Buddhism]] by the government, Tada-in was transformed into a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} enshrining Minamoto no Mitsunaka, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}}, [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} as ''[[kami]]''. The shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Gallery== File:Tada-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, nandaimon.jpg|MInami Daimon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, higashimon.jpg|Higashi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, nishimon.jpg|Nishi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, otorii.jpg|Ōtorii File:Tada-jinja byo.jpg|Grave of Minamoto no Yorimitsu ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Honden|多田神社 本殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156915|title=多田神社 本殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Haiden|多田神社 拝殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/186526|title=多田神社 拝殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Zuishimon|多田神社 随神門||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/123387|title=多田神社 随神門|trans-title=Tada Jinja Zuishinmonlanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Bunsho|多田神社文書||}}, 43 scrolls with 492 document{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/206080|title=多田神社文書|trans-title=Tada Jinja Bunsholanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ===National Tangible Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Treasury|多田神社宝物殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/442906|title=多田神社宝物殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja homotsuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ==See Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)|WD=Q104629006|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Tada revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Tada-jinja]][[en:Tada Shrine]][[fr:Tada-jinja]][[ja:多田神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Tada-jinja]][[en:Tada Shrine]][[fr:Tada-jinja]][[ja:多田神社]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Tada-jinja]][[en:Tada Shrine]][[fr:Tada-jinja]][[ja:多田神社]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Tada Shrine
多田神社 | image = File:Tada Jinja.jpg | caption = ''Honden'' of Tada Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hyōgo Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.86127778|N|135.402625|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Tada Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|WD=Q2531241|1=Minamoto no Mitsunaka}}; {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|1=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}}; {{ill|Minamoto no Yorinobu|WD=Q2699951|1=Minamoto no Yorinobu}}; {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|WD=Q3082851|1=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}}; {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} | founded_by = | established = 978 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Tadain Tadadokorocho, Kawanishi-shi, Hyōgo-ken 666-0127 | website = {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 10 and October 27 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Kawanishi|WD=Q609350|1=Kawanishi, Hyōgo}} }} {{nihongo|'''Tada Shrine'''|多田神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Tadadokoro neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|en|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|WD=Q609350|lt=Kawanishi|1=Kawanishi, Hyōgo}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is the shrine of the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}} clan from which [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and many subsequent {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}} and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' clans claimed descent. This shrine is one of {{ill|Three Genji Shrines|en|Three Genji Shrines|WD=Q7797495|lt=Three Genji Shrines|1=Three Genji Shrines}}, with {{ill|Rokusonnō Shrine|en|Rokusonnō Shrine|WD=Q11392320|lt=Rokusonnō Shrine|1=Rokusonnō Shrine}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} and {{ill|Tsuboi Hachimangū|en|Tsuboi Hachimangū|WD=Q11429677|lt=Tsuboi Hachimangū|1=Tsuboi Hachimangū}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. The shrine was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1951.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1877|title=多田神社|trans-title= Tada Jinja|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tada Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|en|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|WD=Q2531241|lt=Minamoto no Mitsunaka|1=Minamoto no Mitsunaka}}'''| 源満仲|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Tada Daigongen'''| 多田大権現|}} Minamoto no Mitsunaka, also known as Tada no Manjū, is the oldest son of Minamoto no Tsunemoto. Due to his involvement in exposing the Anna Incident, he established himself to be a formidable samurai and was able to earn many governmental positions in various provinces. After serving as governor of Settsu Province, he helped to train various samurais alongside his retainers. Later on in his last few years, Mitsunaka became a Buddhist monk. * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}}'''| 源頼光 |}} Minamoto no Yorimitsu is the eldest son of Mitsunaka. He served the Fujiwara clan and accumulated a great deal of wealth through holding government positions over many provinces. He is most famous for his endeavors with his Four Heavenly Kings in later years, such as the subjugation of Shuten-douji and the extermination of the Tsuchigumo. Yorimitsu inherited his father’s post as governor of Settsu Province and eventually became the founder of the Settsu-Genji. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]'''| 源頼信 |}} Minamoto no Yorinobu is the third son of Mitsunaka. Along with his elder brother Yorimitsu, he served the Fujiwara clan and held the position of Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North. He was noted for having excellent martial prowess, and by suppressing Taira no Tadatsune’s rebellion, he laid the foundation for the Minamoto to advance into the eastern part of the country. In his later years, after serving as the governor of Kawachi Province, he became the founder of the Kawachi-Genji line. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]]'''| 源頼義 |}} Minamoto no Yoriyoshi is the eldest son of Yorinobu, inheriting his father’s excellent skill in martial arts. He gained fame in the eastern part of Japan during his time serving as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a naval base general. He guaranteed the high reputation and prosperity of the Minamoto in the eastern provinces by putting an end to the Early Nine Years’ War. * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}}'''| 源義家 |}} Minamoto no Yoshiie is the eldest son of Yoriyoshi, and he is widely known as “Hachiman-taro” due to his exceptional martial prowess. Following his father’s footsteps, he served as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a military leader in the eastern part of the country. He was able to successfully put an end to the Later Three Years’ War, earning him the title of the “greatest and bravest warrior in the world”. ==History== In 970, {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu-no-kami|1=Settsu Province}} {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|en|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|WD=Q2531241|lt=Minamoto no Mitsunaka|1=Minamoto no Mitsunaka}}, the founder of the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}} clan, received an oracle from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Ōkami]] to established his residence in what is now Kawanishi. A temple (commonly known as Tada-in) was built, as Mitsunaka, converted to Buddhism following the visit of Ingen (954–1028), {{ill|Genshin|en|Genshin|WD=Q1009263|lt=Genshin|1=Genshin}} (942–1017), and Kaku'ub, of the {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} sect. The new convert declared, "You have shown me such extraordinary virtuous things. I'm someone who has killed an immeasurable number of living things. I'd like to atone for these sins. By building a Buddhist Hall I will stop committing any more crimes and save the lives of living things." "What is known as Tada-in is a cluster of halls that began to be built with this one."{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |page=22-29}} When Mitsunaka died in 997 he was buried in Tada-in. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the temple was regarded as the ancestral mausoleum of Seiwa-Genji, but it gradually declined. In the middle of the Kamakura period, [[:en:Hojo Yasutoki|Hojo Yasutoki]], the ''{{ill|Shikken|en|Shikken|WD=Q1045904|lt=Shikken|1=Shikken}}'' of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, became the land steward of Tada-sho ''[[shōen]]'', he decided to carry out a large-scale reconstruction of the Tada-in. In 1273, [[:en:Ninsho|Ninsho]] of [[Saidai-ji]] was appointed to undertake the restoration, and as a result, the temple changed from the Tendai sect to the {{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Risshu|1=Shingon Risshu}} sect. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, who was a descendent of the Seiwa Genji, revered the Tada-in, and temple became a mortuary temple for the {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate}}, with the ashes of successive generations of {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}} buried at the temple. However, the temple was destroyed by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} in 1577. It remained in ruins until restored in 1665 by [[Sakakibara Tadatsugu]] of {{ill|Himeji Domain|en|Himeji Domain|WD=Q1062190|lt=Himeji Domain|1=Himeji Domain}}, {{ill|Inaba Masanori|en|Inaba Masanori|WD=Q6013288|lt=Inaba Masanori|1=Inaba Masanori}} of {{ill|Odawara Domain|en|Odawara Domain|WD=Q903467|lt=Odawara Domain|1=Odawara Domain}} and the descendants of the Tada-in retainers. Shogun {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsuna|en|Tokugawa Ietsuna|WD=Q323422|lt=Tokugawa Ietsuna|1=Tokugawa Ietsuna}} granted an estate with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' of 500 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'' for its upkeep, and most of its structures were rebuilt by 1667. As with the Ashikaga, successive generation of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa shogun]]s ashes and ''[[:en:Ihai|Ihai]]'' mortuary tablets with enshrined at the temple. Shogun [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] restored the main shrine building in 1695. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, due to the [[shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shintoism and Buddhism]] by the government, Tada-in was transformed into a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} enshrining Minamoto no Mitsunaka, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}}, [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} as ''[[kami]]''. The shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Gallery== File:Tada-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, nandaimon.jpg|MInami Daimon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, higashimon.jpg|Higashi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, nishimon.jpg|Nishi-mon (兵庫県指定文化財) File:Tada-jinja, otorii.jpg|Ōtorii File:Tada-jinja byo.jpg|Grave of Minamoto no Yorimitsu ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Honden|多田神社 本殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/156915|title=多田神社 本殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Honden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Haiden|多田神社 拝殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/186526|title=多田神社 拝殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja Heiden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Zuishimon|多田神社 随神門||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/123387|title=多田神社 随神門|trans-title=Tada Jinja Zuishinmonlanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Bunsho|多田神社文書||}}, 43 scrolls with 492 document{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/206080|title=多田神社文書|trans-title=Tada Jinja Bunsholanguage=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ===National Tangible Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|Tada Shrine Treasury|多田神社宝物殿||}}, built in 1667{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/442906|title=多田神社宝物殿|trans-title=Tada Jinja homotsuden|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=20 August 2020}} ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)|WD=Q104629006|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Hyōgo)}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Tada 多田神社 | image = File:Tada Jinja.jpg | caption = ''Honden'' of Tada Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Hyōgo Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.86127778|N|135.402625|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Tada Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity ={{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|WD=Q2531241|1=Minamoto no Mitsunaka}}; {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|1=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}}; {{ill|Minamoto no Yorinobu|WD=Q2699951|1=Minamoto no Yorinobu}}; {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|WD=Q3082851|1=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}}; {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} | founded_by = | established = 978 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Tadain Tadadokorocho, Kawanishi-shi, Hyōgo-ken 666-0127 | website = {{Official|1=http://www.tadajinjya.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = April 10 and October 27 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Kawanishi|WD=Q609350|1=Kawanishi, Hyōgo}} }} {{nihongo|'''Tada Shrine'''|多田神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the Tadadokoro neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|en|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|WD=Q609350|lt=Kawanishi|1=Kawanishi, Hyōgo}} in {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The shrine is also called ‘Tada-Daigongen-Sha’ or ‘Kansai Nikko’, literally, ‘Nikko of Western Japan’. This shrine is the shrine of the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}} clan from which [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] and many subsequent {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}} and ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' clans claimed descent. This shrine is one of {{ill|Three Genji Shrines|en|Three Genji Shrines|WD=Q7797495|lt=Three Genji Shrines|1=Three Genji Shrines}}, with {{ill|Rokusonnō Shrine|en|Rokusonnō Shrine|WD=Q11392320|lt=Rokusonnō Shrine|1=Rokusonnō Shrine}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} and {{ill|Tsuboi Hachimangū|en|Tsuboi Hachimangū|WD=Q11429677|lt=Tsuboi Hachimangū|1=Tsuboi Hachimangū}} in {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. The shrine was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1951.{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1877|title=多田神社|trans-title= Tada Jinja|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Tada Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|en|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|WD=Q2531241|lt=Minamoto no Mitsunaka|1=Minamoto no Mitsunaka}}'''| 源満仲|}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Tada Daigongen'''| 多田大権現|}} Minamoto no Mitsunaka, also known as Tada no Manjū, is the oldest son of Minamoto no Tsunemoto. Due to his involvement in exposing the Anna Incident, he established himself to be a formidable samurai and was able to earn many governmental positions in various provinces. After serving as governor of Settsu Province, he helped to train various samurais alongside his retainers. Later on in his last few years, Mitsunaka became a Buddhist monk. * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}}'''| 源頼光 |}} Minamoto no Yorimitsu is the eldest son of Mitsunaka. He served the Fujiwara clan and accumulated a great deal of wealth through holding government positions over many provinces. He is most famous for his endeavors with his Four Heavenly Kings in later years, such as the subjugation of Shuten-douji and the extermination of the Tsuchigumo. Yorimitsu inherited his father’s post as governor of Settsu Province and eventually became the founder of the Settsu-Genji. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]'''| 源頼信 |}} Minamoto no Yorinobu is the third son of Mitsunaka. Along with his elder brother Yorimitsu, he served the Fujiwara clan and held the position of Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North. He was noted for having excellent martial prowess, and by suppressing Taira no Tadatsune’s rebellion, he laid the foundation for the Minamoto to advance into the eastern part of the country. In his later years, after serving as the governor of Kawachi Province, he became the founder of the Kawachi-Genji line. * {{nihongo|'''[[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]]'''| 源頼義 |}} Minamoto no Yoriyoshi is the eldest son of Yorinobu, inheriting his father’s excellent skill in martial arts. He gained fame in the eastern part of Japan during his time serving as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a naval base general. He guaranteed the high reputation and prosperity of the Minamoto in the eastern provinces by putting an end to the Early Nine Years’ War. * {{nihongo|'''{{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}}'''| 源義家 |}} Minamoto no Yoshiie is the eldest son of Yoriyoshi, and he is widely known as “Hachiman-taro” due to his exceptional martial prowess. Following his father’s footsteps, he served as Commander-in-Chief of the Defense of the North and as a military leader in the eastern part of the country. He was able to successfully put an end to the Later Three Years’ War, earning him the title of the “greatest and bravest warrior in the world”. ==History== In 970, {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu-no-kami|1=Settsu Province}} {{ill|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|en|Minamoto no Mitsunaka|WD=Q2531241|lt=Minamoto no Mitsunaka|1=Minamoto no Mitsunaka}}, the founder of the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}} clan, received an oracle from [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Ōkami]] to established his residence in what is now Kawanishi. A temple (commonly known as Tada-in) was built, as Mitsunaka, converted to Buddhism following the visit of Ingen (954–1028), {{ill|Genshin|en|Genshin|WD=Q1009263|lt=Genshin|1=Genshin}} (942–1017), and Kaku'ub, of the {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} sect. The new convert declared, "You have shown me such extraordinary virtuous things. I'm someone who has killed an immeasurable number of living things. I'd like to atone for these sins. By building a Buddhist Hall I will stop committing any more crimes and save the lives of living things." "What is known as Tada-in is a cluster of halls that began to be built with this one."{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |page=22-29}} When Mitsunaka died in 997 he was buried in Tada-in. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the temple was regarded as the ancestral mausoleum of Seiwa-Genji, but it gradually declined. In the middle of the Kamakura period, [[:en:Hojo Yasutoki|Hojo Yasutoki]], the ''{{ill|Shikken|en|Shikken|WD=Q1045904|lt=Shikken|1=Shikken}}'' of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, became the land steward of Tada-sho ''[[shōen]]'', he decided to carry out a large-scale reconstruction of the Tada-in. In 1273, [[:en:Ninsho|Ninsho]] of [[Saidai-ji]] was appointed to undertake the restoration, and as a result, the temple changed from the Tendai sect to the {{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Risshu|1=Shingon Risshu}} sect. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, who was a descendent of the Seiwa Genji, revered the Tada-in, and temple became a mortuary temple for the {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate}}, with the ashes of successive generations of {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}} buried at the temple. However, the temple was destroyed by the forces of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} in 1577. It remained in ruins until restored in 1665 by [[Sakakibara Tadatsugu]] of {{ill|Himeji Domain|en|Himeji Domain|WD=Q1062190|lt=Himeji Domain|1=Himeji Domain}}, {{ill|Inaba Masanori|en|Inaba Masanori|WD=Q6013288|lt=Inaba Masanori|1=Inaba Masanori}} of {{ill|Odawara Domain|en|Odawara Domain|WD=Q903467|lt=Odawara Domain|1=Odawara Domain}} and the descendants of the Tada-in retainers. Shogun {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsuna|en|Tokugawa Ietsuna|WD=Q323422|lt=Tokugawa Ietsuna|1=Tokugawa Ietsuna}} granted an estate with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' of 500 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'' for its upkeep, and most of its structures were rebuilt by 1667. As with the Ashikaga, successive generation of the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa shogun]]s ashes and ''[[:en:Ihai|Ihai]]'' mortuary tablets with enshrined at the temple. Shogun [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]] restored the main shrine building in 1695. In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, due to the [[shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shintoism and Buddhism]] by the government, Tada-in was transformed into a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} enshrining Minamoto no Mitsunaka, {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}}, [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]], [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], and {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} as ''[[kami]]''. The shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Gallery== File:Tada-jinja, haiden-2.jpg|Haiden (ICP) File:Tada-jinja, zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon
